primary_diagnosis.name: Severe acute allergic contact dermatitis (plant/Rhus-induced) of the dorsal forearms and hands, with vesiculobullous change following garden brush exposure
primary_diagnosis.snomed_display: Allergic contact dermatitis caused by plant

differential.evidence_basis: Intensely pruritic, burning, vesiculobullous eruption in linear/geometric streaks tracing plant contact, onset ~12 hours after clearing brush, confined to exposed dorsal forearms/hands — the classic delayed (type IV) plant contact dermatitis pattern.

differential.diagnosis: Severe mucocutaneous drug reaction (Stevens-Johnson Syndrome / toxic epidermal necrolysis)
differential.evidence_basis: A blistering eruption always warrants screening for SJS/TEN; must be excluded by asking about mucosal (mouth/eye/genital) involvement, skin pain/detachment, systemic upset and a recent new drug — none of which can be fully examined for over telehealth.

differential.diagnosis: Secondary bacterial infection / cellulitis of the excoriated bullae
differential.evidence_basis: Ruptured, weeping, excoriated bullae are a portal for bacterial infection; must be actively screened for (spreading erythema, heat, purulence, fever) as it cannot be palpated for remotely.

Also vesicular, but typically unilateral in a single dermatome with neuropathic pain predominating; here the eruption is bilateral, symmetrical, follows exposure geometry not a dermatome, and itch predominates over pain.
Can produce bullae, but is usually not intensely pruritic, tends to be clustered with honey-crusting rather than linear, and lacks the clear plant-exposure geometry.

Bullous pemphigoid is a subacute/chronic, often widespread tense-blister disease of older adults; the hyper-acute onset, direct correlation with a single environmental exposure and localisation to exposed skin are incompatible.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening the leading diagnosis is a plant allergic contact dermatitis; the important-not-to-miss considerations are SJS/TEN and secondary bacterial infection, which drive the telehealth red-flag screen and the safety-net rather than the baseline tier.

differential.evidence_basis: No mucosal involvement, no skin detachment, no new drug, no systemic upset and no secondary-infection features — a localised plant contact dermatitis, not a systemic or infective blistering disorder.

differential.diagnosis: Severe mucocutaneous drug reaction (Stevens-Johnson Syndrome / TEN)
differential.evidence_basis: Screened negative now (no mucosal lesions, no skin pain/detachment, no new drug, systemically well) but remains the catastrophic mimic to keep a safety-net for; cannot be fully examined remotely.

differential.diagnosis: Secondary bacterial infection / cellulitis of the excoriated bullae
differential.evidence_basis: Absent now (afebrile, clear exudate, no spreading heat) but a foreseeable complication of ruptured excoriated bullae — a mandatory safety-net target, not a current diagnosis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The red-flag screen removes anaphylaxis, SJS/TEN and secondary infection and confirms a telehealth/GP-manageable plant contact dermatitis. The severity/blistering justifies prompt review to obtain a short oral corticosteroid course, but NOT urgent same-day ED or 000.

differential.diagnosis: Severe acute allergic contact dermatitis (plant/Rhus-induced) of the exposed forearms/hands
differential.evidence_basis: Classic plant contact dermatitis geometry and time-course; systemically well with negative anaphylaxis, SJS/TEN and secondary-infection screens; the severity and blistering drive symptom relief, potent topical corticosteroid, urushiol decontamination and prompt review for a short oral steroid course rather than emergency escalation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct end-point is recognition of a common plant allergic contact dermatitis, patient-performable measures (cool compresses, do-not-scratch, wash off residual plant oils), potent topical corticosteroid, and PROMPT review to obtain a short oral corticosteroid course for the severe blistering — with explicit safety-netting for anaphylaxis, SJS/TEN and secondary bacterial infection. Reflexive escalation to 000/ED is over-triage.

red_flags.description: Evolving systemic allergic reaction / anaphylaxis or airway angioedema — facial, lip, tongue or throat swelling, difficulty breathing, widespread urticaria, or faintness.
red_flags.clinical_significance: The one immediately life-threatening pathway a distressed patient and triager fear; airway angioedema/anaphylaxis needs adrenaline and an ambulance. Breath-Ezy cannot give IM adrenaline, so recognition and 000 escalation is the correct action.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unrecognised airway angioedema/anaphylaxis can cause airway obstruction and death.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial infection / cellulitis of the excoriated bullae — spreading erythema and heat beyond the affected skin, thick yellow or foul-smelling pus, fever or rigors.
red_flags.clinical_significance: Ruptured, weeping, scratched bullae are a portal for bacterial infection; established cellulitis needs oral (or IV) antibiotics and in-person assessment and cannot be palpated for over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An untreated spreading cellulitis could progress to systemic infection.

red_flags.description: Severe mucocutaneous reaction (Stevens-Johnson Syndrome / toxic epidermal necrolysis) — mucosal (mouth/eye/genital) erosions, painful skin detachment/peeling beyond exposed areas, or systemic upset, particularly with a recent new drug.
red_flags.clinical_significance: A dermatological emergency with high mortality; blistering eruptions must be screened for mucosal involvement and a drug trigger, which cannot be fully examined for remotely.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed SJS/TEN progresses to extensive epidermal loss with a high risk of death; delays are catastrophic.

pathophysiology_summary: Allergic contact dermatitis is a delayed (type IV, T-cell-mediated) hypersensitivity reaction. In plant/Rhus dermatitis the allergen is urushiol, an oleoresin transferred to the skin on contact with the plant (or contaminated tools/clothing). In a previously sensitised person, re-exposure triggers sensitised T-cells to drive a brisk eczematous response over roughly 12-48 hours, producing intensely pruritic erythema with vesiculation and, when severe, tense bullae. The distribution mirrors the mechanics of exposure: linear and geometric streaks along the exact lines where plant matter dragged across the skin, confined to the exposed dorsal forearms and hands. The eruption is a localised cutaneous inflammatory process, not a systemic or infective one — there is no mucosal involvement, no airway compromise and no systemic upset. The clinical severity (extensive bullae, sleep-preventing pruritus, functional impairment of the hands) is what justifies a short course of systemic corticosteroid in addition to potent topical steroid, cool compresses and antihistamine — while the ruptured, excoriated bullae create a portal for secondary bacterial infection, and the blistering itself mandates a screen for the catastrophic mimics (anaphylaxis/airway angioedema and SJS/TEN) before committing to outpatient management.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being swept along by the 'giant blisters', unbearable itch, rapid spread and the triage note's bullous-pemphigoid/SJS/systemic-allergy framing into calling 000 / sending to ED, when the red-flag screen for anaphylaxis, SJS/TEN and secondary infection is negative and a common plant contact dermatitis is telehealth/GP-manageable.

Treating vesiculobullous change as automatically dangerous rather than recognising that severe allergic contact dermatitis routinely blisters; the exposure geometry, itch predominance and absent systemic features point firmly to a benign cause.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Missing the SJS/TEN and anaphylaxis screen
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Correctly labelling contact dermatitis but never asking about mucosal involvement, skin detachment, a new drug, or facial/airway swelling — omitting the one screen that separates a benign blistering rash from a lethal mimic that telehealth cannot examine for.

Recommending topical antihistamine or topical local-anaesthetic 'itch' creams, which are themselves common contact sensitisers and can worsen an allergic contact dermatitis — oral antihistamine and topical corticosteroid are the appropriate choices.
Prescribing empiric oral antibiotics for clear serous weeping and crusting with no fever, spreading induration or purulence — treating normal exudate/serous crust as infection.
Failing to advise washing skin, clothing and tools to remove residual plant oil, allowing continued or repeated exposure that perpetuates the reaction.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Direct examination of the vesicles/bullae, mucous membranes and skin integrity to definitively exclude SJS/TEN or early secondary infection
Palpation for warmth, induration or tenderness of an early cellulitis beyond the visible redness
Exact blood pressure, heart rate, temperature and respiratory rate without a home device (source in-person values BP 135/85, HR 90, Temp 36.8, RR 16, SpO2 99%, Wt 72 kg)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a plant allergic contact dermatitis from the exposure history and video; instruct patient-performable measures now (cool damp compresses instead of scratching, do not pop the blisters, wash skin/clothing/tools to remove residual plant oil, an oral antihistamine for the itch); advise a potent topical corticosteroid; and arrange PROMPT review (in person or telehealth, within ~24-48 hours) to obtain a short course of oral corticosteroid for the severe blistering and to lay eyes on the skin. Escalation to 000/ED is reserved for the red-flag safety-net triggers (airway/anaphylaxis, SJS/TEN, spreading secondary infection), not the baseline.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the diagnosis (the exposure geometry and time-course are classic). The dominant risks are OVER-action (reflexively escalating a distressing but benign blistering rash to ED/000) and UNDER-screening the blistering mimics (anaphylaxis, SJS/TEN) and the secondary-infection risk that telehealth cannot examine for.
